title: Right side view of Binary tree

content.problemStatement:
Given the rootrootroot of a binary tree, imagine standing on the **right side** of the tree. Return an array of the values of the nodes you can see ordered from top to bottom.

**Notes:**


Return the rightmost node value at each depth level (top → bottom).
Expected solutions commonly use BFS level-order traversal or DFS with depth tracking.
Function Declaration

Function Name



rightSideViewrightSideViewrightSideView — This function returns the list of node values visible when viewing a binary tree from the right side.



Parameters



rootrootroot: A pointer to the root node of the binary tree.
The binary tree may contain 000 to 100100100 nodes, and each node contains an integer value or may be null (`-1` in level-order input).



Return Value



Returns an array containing the values of the rightmost nodes at each depth level of the binary tree.
The values are ordered from **top to bottom**, representing the nodes visible from the right side.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two lines of input: The first line contains a single integer N - size of the array that contains nodes and empty leaf nodes. The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the level-order traversal of the tree, where -1 denotes a null child.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the right side view of the binary tree — a sequence of space-separated integers representing the values of the nodes visible from the right side.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first test case:

    10
   /  \
  5    15
 / \     \
3   7     20

From the right side you see 10, then 15, then 20.

Explanation:
For the test case:

    1
   / \
  2   3

From the right side you see 1, then 3.

Explanation:
For the test case:

    1
     \
      2
       \
        3

From the right side you see 1, then 2, then 3.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we use a level order traversal (BFS) approach. We start by creating a queue and adding the root node to it. Then, we enter a loop where we keep removing nodes from the queue and adding their children until the queue is empty. We keep track of the number of nodes at each level and add the last node at each level to the result.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, O(T * N) total. The precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the queue used for BFS.
